runs.interpretation: Intertemporal elasticity of substitution (IES) for nondurable consumption, defined as the elasticity of the growth rate of nondurable consumption to the intertemporal price of consumption (annual macro-calibration, distinct from risk aversion) for a representative U.S. household.

runs.reasoningSummary: I put most mass on sub-to-moderate IES values (around 0.5–1) because calibrated annual-macro models typically require limited sensitivity of consumption growth to interest-rate/intertemporal price changes, while higher IES values are sometimes consistent with some asset-pricing/macro moments but are less robust. The lower tail allows for fairly inelastic consumption growth responses, and the upper tail allows for scenarios with stronger substitution.
